figures.caption: Figure 1. Hypotheses regarding relationships between path integration and spatial updating 14 In the experimental paradigm, the participant moves along a predetermined trajectory (the bolded 15 solid curve, while keeping track of the start position of the trajectory (path integration) or a target 16 position not on the trajectory (spatial updating). Also refer to Figure 2 for a detailed description of the 17 task. 18

Figure 6: Main Effect of Task Type on BOLD Responses in the Univariate FMRI Analysis 2 (A) Precuneus was more strongly activated in spatial updating trials than path integration trials, 3 regardless of dot density. (B) Middle frontal gyrus (dorsal premotor cortex) was more strongly 4 activated in spatial updating trials than path integration trials, regardless of dot density. Results are 5 displayed on the MNI template at a threshold of puncorrected < 0.001. In the right panels, we extracted 6 mean beta estimates (in arbitrary units) for the significant clusters, which are plotted as a function of 7 task type and dot density. Error bars represent SE of the mean. 8 9

Table 2: Main Effect of Dot Density in the Univariate fMRI Analysis 5 Spatial coordinates of the local maxima in the group analysis (p < 0.05 corrected). These regions 6 showed higher activation for high vs. low dot density, independent of the task. Multiple comparisons 7 were corrected across the entire brain, using the parametric cluster-based analysis (voxel-defining 8 threshold: puncorrected < 0.001). Cluster size is determined with voxel-wise puncorrected < 0.001. RH/LH – 9 right/left hemisphere. 10 11

Figure 8. PPI Results with the Precuneus Cluster as the Seed Region 2 The seed region here corresponds to the precuneus cluster displayed in Figure 6A, which displayed 3 stronger activation during spatial updating compared to path integration (lower row). In the upper 4 row, PPI results are displayed on the MNI template at a threshold of puncorrected < 0.001. In the middle 5 row, scatterplots depict the temporal relationship between brain activity between regions of interest 6 for each task type, based on the first eigenvariates of the time-series BOLD signals for these clusters. 7 8 9

Table 3. PPI Results with the Precuneus Cluster as the Seed Region 13 Using the precuneus cluster (Figure 6A) as the seed region, three clusters of voxels showed stronger 14 connectivity with the precuneus in path integration trials than spatial updating trials. The table shows 15 the spatial coordinates of the local maxima in the group analysis (p <. 05 corrected). Multiple 16 comparisons are corrected across the entire brain, using the parametric cluster-based analysis (voxel-17 defining threshold: puncorrected < 0.001). Cluster size is determined with voxel-wise puncorrected < 0.001. 18 RH/LH – right/left hemisphere. 19 20

Figure 5: Behavioral Results of the FMRI experiment 17 (A) Absolute pointing error is plotted as a function of task type and turning angle. The main effect of 18 turning angle was significant, meaning that absolute pointing error increased as turning angle 19 increased, regardless of task type. (B) Response time is plotted as a function of task type and turning 20 angle. The main effect of task type was significant, meaning that response time was longer in path 21 integration than spatial updating, regardless of turning angle. In both plots, bar height represents the 22 mean and error bars represent ±SEM. 23 24 To check for systematic changes in pointing performance over the course of the 25 experiment, we computed the correlation between absolute pointing error and trial number 26 for each participant and each of the four conditions. We then transformed those correlations 27 to normally distributed variables using Fisher's z-transformation, averaged the z-values across 28

Figure 2. Experimental Setup for the Behavioral Experiment and the FMRI Experiment 13 (A) Paths used in the task. The yellow dot represents the starting position of all the paths (i.e, start). 14 Participants were required to keep track of the start location in path integration trials. The red dots 15 represent to-be-tracked locations (i.e., target) in the spatial updating trials for different paths. Note 16 that for each path, the correct pointing angle to the red dot (target) and the correct pointing angle to 17 the yellow dot (start) are matched in magnitude. (B-D) Demonstration of a spatial updating trial. First, 18 a target object (yellow square) appears (B). The target object disappeared once the movement starts 19 (C). After the movement has ended, the participant is instructed to point to the location of the target 20 object (D). Refer to Methods for a detailed description of the task trial. 21 22

Figure 4. Gaze Position in the Behavioral Experiment 2 Average horizontal gaze position during the motion phase for spatial updating (red) and path 3 integration (blue) trials for the different paths. The screen position of where the target object had 4 initially been presented is indicated by the grey line. 5 6

Figure 3. Behavioral Performance and Eye-Tracking Results in the Behavioral Experiment 2 (A) Absolute pointing error increased as a function of turning angle during the outbound path. (B) 3 Average response time was shorter for spatial updating than for path integration. (C) Average 4 horizontal gaze position as a function of turning angle. (D) Absolute horizontal distance between gaze 5 and object location. In all plots, bar height represents the mean and error bars represent ±SEM. 6 7 8

Figure 7: Main Effect of Dot Density on BOLD Responses in the Univariate FMRI Analysis 2 (A) V1 showed stronger activation for high- than low-dot-density trials, irrespective of task type. The 3 left panel shows the statistical map projected onto the MNI template at a threshold of puncorrected < 4 0.001. The right panel plots mean activation estimates, averaged across voxels, within the significant 5 cluster, as a function of task type and dot density. (B) Hippocampus showed stronger activation for 6 high- than low-dot-density trials, irrespective of task type, following the same format as in (A). (C) 7 hMT+ activation was not modulated by dot density. The left panel displays the anatomical hMT+ mask 8 for one example participant. The right panel displays mean activation estimates (averaged across 9 voxels) from a ROI-based analysis using participant-specific hMT+ masks, plotted as a function of task 10 type and dot density. All other aspects are as in (A) and (B). Error bars represent SE of the mean. 11 12

Table 1: Main Effect of Task Type in the Univariate FMRI Analysis 11 Using the parametric cluster-based analysis (voxel defining threshold puncorrected < 0.001), we observed 12 two significant clusters with stronger activation in spatial updating than path integration trials. Cluster 13 size was defined with voxel-wise puncorrected < 0.001. RH/LH – right/left hemisphere. 14 15

Figure 9. Results of the Whole-Brain Analysis of Inter-Regional Functional Connectivity 6 Pattern 7 (A) Nodes used in the analysis, which are from Power et al. (2011). (B) Results of the inter-8 region functional connectivity pattern classification analysis. The blue bars represent the 9 surrogate distribution of classification accuracy, which was obtained by randomizing the task 10 labels of the trials. The orange bar represents the true classification accuracy (= 0.844), with 11 a significance level of p1-tailed = 0.0002. 12 13 14
